chunk_summary: Guidance on choking prevention and texture progression during feeding skill development, emphasizing safe food preparation, texture transitions, and caregiver practices for infants and toddlers.
learning_objectives: Identify high-risk choking foods and appropriate modifications for toddlers.
Understand texture progression milestones from 6 to 12 months to support chewing development.
Recognize signs of unsafe feeding responses and differentiate them from normal gagging.
Learn practical food preparation techniques and choking first aid for caregivers.
key_facts: Choking risk increases during self-feeding; avoid whole nuts, grapes, popcorn, and sticky clumps like peanut butter.
Texture progression: thick purees at 6 months, soft lumps and finger foods by 8–10 months, family foods by 12 months.
Delaying lumpy textures beyond late infancy can increase gagging and food refusal.
Signs of unsafe feeding include repeated coughing, wet voice, or breathing changes.
Follow IDDSI texture definitions for dysphagia risk instead of informal labels like 'semi-solid.'
reference_quote: Children should eat seated upright with close supervision, not while playing, crying, or lying down. (Source: CDC)
